title: π_0: A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General Robot Control
problem: Robot learning still struggles with data scarcity, generalization, and robustness: narrowly trained policies rarely see enough tasks, environments, mistakes, and recoveries to behave like flexible real-world systems.
General-purpose vision-language models bring broad semantic knowledge, but they are not grounded in physical interaction or continuous robot control.

Dexterous manipulation requires precise, high-frequency, multimodal action distributions across heterogeneous robot embodiments, which is difficult for standard token-prediction VLMs or single-task policies.
solution: The paper introduces \u03c0_0, a vision-language-action policy that starts from a pre-trained PaliGemma VLM and adds robot-specific state and action pathways, including a smaller action expert with separate weights for proprioception and continuous actions (5a).

Instead of predicting one discrete action token, \u03c0_0 models an H=50 future action chunk with conditional flow matching: the action expert learns a denoising vector field **4a**, then samples actions by Euler integration **4b** for precise continuous control.

The base policy is pre-trained on a weighted cross-embodiment mixture: the authors combine their dexterous manipulation data from 7 robot configurations and 68 tasks with open robot datasets, padding action/state dimensions and masking missing cameras so one model can cover many platforms (5b).

The training recipe mirrors large-model pre-training and post-training: broad data teaches coverage and recovery behaviors, while curated task-specific fine-tuning teaches efficient, fluent execution; high-level VLM policies can also issue language subcommands for semantically complex tasks.
key_concepts: VLM initialization matters because the robot policy inherits object, scene, and instruction understanding from Internet-scale image-text pre-training, then grounds those representations in embodied action through robot data.

The action expert is a mixture-of-experts-style extension of the transformer: image and language tokens use the VLM backbone, while state and noisy action tokens use a smaller robotics expert that still communicates through self-attention (5a).

Flow matching learns how to move a noisy action chunk toward a plausible expert action chunk rather than choosing a single mean action. This is important for dexterous manipulation, where the same observation can admit several valid motions and where temporally coherent chunks reduce control jitter **4a**.

Cross-embodiment pre-training treats different robots as samples from one large conditional action distribution. The mixture in (5b) exposes \u03c0_0 to more environments, robot kinematics, task phases, and recovery behaviors than a single specialized dataset.

math.label: conditional flow-matching objective
math.latex: L^{\tau}(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{p(A_t\mid o_t),\,q(A_t^{\tau}\mid A_t)}\left[\left\|v_{\theta}(A_t^{\tau},o_t)-u(A_t^{\tau}\mid A_t)\right\|^2\right],\quad q(A_t^{\tau}\mid A_t)=\mathcal{N}(\tau A_t,(1-\tau)I)
